modules.title: Leadership & People Management
modules.description: Learn to lead a team, delegate well, and help other people do their best work — even if you're not a manager yet.

children.title: Leadership Fundamentals
children.description: What leadership actually means day to day, and why it's a skill you can build early, not just a title.

links.title: What Makes a Leader — HBR Classic
Submit your leadership self-reflection
links.description: In a Google Doc, describe one moment (in college, a project, a family situation, anywhere) where you naturally stepped up to lead — even informally. Write 300-400 words on what you did, what worked, and what you'd do differently now. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Delegation & Accountability
children.description: Learn to hand off work effectively without micromanaging or dropping the ball.

links.title: How to Hold People Accountable Without Being a Jerk
Submit a delegation plan
links.description: Pick a real or hypothetical group project with 3-4 people. In a Google Doc, break the work into clear tasks, assign each to a person based on their strengths, define what 'done' looks like for each task, and describe how you'd check in without micromanaging. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Giving and Receiving Feedback
children.description: Say hard things kindly, and take feedback without getting defensive.

links.title: SBI Feedback Model — Center for Creative Leadership
Submit written feedback examples
links.description: Using the Situation-Behavior-Impact model, write 2 pieces of feedback in a Google Doc: one piece of constructive feedback you'd genuinely give a teammate, and one piece of positive feedback. For each, also write how you'd want to receive similar feedback yourself. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Leading a Team Project
children.description: Apply everything by actually leading a small project from start to finish.

links.title: Submit your project retrospective
links.description: Lead a real small group task — a college assignment, a hackathon team, a club event, or a study group. Afterward, write a retrospective in a Google Doc: what the goal was, how you split up work, one thing you'd repeat as a leader, and one thing you'd change. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

modules.title: Emotional Intelligence & Resilience
modules.description: Understand and manage your own emotions, handle conflict well, and bounce back from setbacks.

children.title: Understanding Emotional Intelligence
children.description: The four core skills of EQ: self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, and relationship management.

links.title: What is Emotional Intelligence? — Harvard Business Publishing
Emotional Intelligence in Leadership — Daniel Goleman Summary
Submit your self-awareness reflection
links.description: In a Google Doc, write 300-400 words identifying: a situation that reliably triggers a strong emotional reaction in you, what you usually do in that moment, and one alternative response you could try next time. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Managing Stress in Demanding Environments
children.description: Practical, sustainable ways to stay steady under deadlines, exams, or high-pressure work.

links.title: Stress Management Techniques — American Psychological Association
Preventing Burnout Before It Starts — HBR
Submit your personal stress-management toolkit
links.description: In a Google Doc, list 5 specific, realistic things you can do when you notice you're stressed or overwhelmed (e.g. short walk, calling a friend, breaking a task down, stepping away for 10 minutes). Also note early warning signs that tell you stress is building. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Conflict Resolution
children.description: Disagree productively instead of avoiding conflict or letting it become personal.

links.title: How to Manage Conflict at Work — HBR
Submit a conflict resolution case study
links.description: Describe a real disagreement you were part of (group project, family, friends) in a Google Doc. Write: what each side wanted, what was actually driving the disagreement underneath, how it was resolved (or wasn't), and what a better resolution could have looked like. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Building Resilience
children.description: Recover from setbacks — a rejected application, a failed project, harsh feedback — without spiraling.

links.title: What is Resilience? — American Psychological Association
Carol Dweck on Growth Mindset — Stanford
Submit your setback reflection
links.description: Write about a real setback you've faced (a rejection, a failed exam, a project that didn't work) in a Google Doc — 300-400 words. Cover what happened, how you responded at the time, and what you'd tell a friend going through the same thing today. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

modules.title: Financial Literacy & Money Management
modules.description: The practical money skills that college rarely teaches — budgeting, salary basics, and how investing actually works.

children.title: Personal Budgeting Basics
children.description: Track where your money actually goes and build a simple, realistic budget.

links.title: How to Make a Budget — NerdWallet
Submit your monthly budget sheet
links.description: Create a Google Sheet tracking your actual income and expenses for one month (or a realistic projected month if you don't have income yet). Categorize spending, and note one category you'd want to adjust going forward. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Understanding Salary, Taxes & Offers
children.description: Read a job offer like someone who knows what they're looking at.

links.title: How to Read a Job Offer Letter
Submit your offer breakdown
links.description: Find a real sample tech job offer letter or CTC breakdown online (search 'sample CTC breakdown India tech job'). In a Google Doc, explain in your own words what each component means (base salary, bonus, benefits, deductions) and what the realistic monthly in-hand amount would be. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Saving & Investing Fundamentals
children.description: The basic concepts behind saving consistently and letting money grow over time.

links.title: The Power of Compound Interest — Investopedia
Submit your investing basics summary
links.description: In a Google Doc, explain in your own words (250-350 words): the difference between saving and investing, what compound interest means with a simple example, and 3 common beginner investment options in India (e.g. fixed deposits, mutual funds, index funds) with one pro and one con for each. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

modules.title: Negotiation Skills
modules.description: Learn to negotiate confidently — for a salary, a freelance rate, or anything else.

children.title: Negotiation Fundamentals
children.description: The core principles behind any good negotiation — interests vs positions, BATNA, and anchoring.

links.title: Getting to Yes — Key Concepts Summary
Submit your negotiation concepts summary
links.description: In a Google Doc, explain in your own words (250-350 words) what BATNA means, the difference between interests and positions, and one real or hypothetical negotiation situation where knowing your BATNA would have changed your approach. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Salary & Freelance Rate Negotiation Practice
children.description: Practice the actual conversation before you need to have it for real.

links.title: How to Negotiate Your Salary — Ramit Sethi (freeCodeCamp summary)
Submit a recorded salary negotiation role-play
links.description: With a friend playing the recruiter, record a 3-5 minute role-play where you're given a lower-than-expected offer and need to negotiate. Practice stating your case calmly with a specific number and reasoning, not just asking for 'more'. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the URL here.

modules.title: Entrepreneurship & Product Thinking
modules.description: Think like a founder — validate ideas, build MVPs, and pitch clearly, even if you plan to stay an employee.

children.title: Idea Validation & Customer Discovery
children.description: Learn to test whether an idea is worth building before you write a single line of code.

links.title: The Mom Test — Key Ideas Summary
How to Talk to Users — Y Combinator
Submit your customer discovery notes
links.description: Pick a real problem you think is worth solving. Interview at least 3 real people who have that problem (friends and family count) about how they currently deal with it — not whether they'd use your solution. In a Google Doc, summarize each conversation and what you learned that surprised you. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Building an MVP
children.description: Build the smallest version of an idea that still tests the core assumption.

links.title: What is an MVP? — Y Combinator Startup School
Submit your MVP repo or link
links.description: Build the smallest possible version of the idea you validated in the previous module — a landing page with a signup form, a no-code prototype, or a barebones working app. It should be good enough to show a real person and get a reaction. Push it to a public repo (or paste a live link in the repo's README). Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Pitching Your Idea
children.description: Explain your idea clearly and convincingly in under 3 minutes.

links.title: How to Pitch Your Startup — Y Combinator
Submit your pitch video
links.description: Record a 2-3 minute pitch of your idea covering: the problem, who has it, your solution, and why now. Speak directly to camera, no slides required. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the URL here.

children.title: Basics of Business Models
children.description: Understand how companies actually make money — before you build something nobody will pay for.

links.title: Submit your business model canvas
links.description: Fill out a Business Model Canvas (a template is fine, or recreate the boxes in a Google Doc/Slides) for your MVP idea or any product you use daily. Cover at least: value proposition, customer segments, revenue streams, and cost structure. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

modules.title: Design Thinking & UI/UX Fundamentals
modules.description: Learn to design software that people actually enjoy using, even if you're primarily a developer.

children.title: The Design Thinking Process
children.description: Empathize, define, ideate, prototype, test — the five-stage loop behind good design.

links.title: An Introduction to Design Thinking — Stanford d.school
Submit a design thinking walkthrough
links.description: Pick an everyday app or object you find frustrating. In a Google Doc, walk through all 5 design thinking stages applied to redesigning it: who you'd empathize with, the problem definition, 3 ideas, a rough prototype description, and how you'd test it. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Wireframing & Prototyping with Figma
children.description: Turn ideas into clickable mockups before writing any code.

links.title: Laws of UX — Design Principles Reference
Submit your Figma prototype link
links.description: Design a clickable prototype in Figma for a small app idea (3-5 screens) — a to-do app, a food ordering flow, or a redesign of an app you use. Include at least one interactive click-through between screens. Set sharing to 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the Figma link here.

children.title: Usability Testing Basics
children.description: Watch a real person use your design and learn what actually confuses them.

links.title: How to Conduct a Usability Test — NN/g
Submit your usability test findings
links.description: Give your Figma prototype from the previous module to 2-3 people who haven't seen it, and ask them to complete a specific task without help. Watch where they get stuck or confused. In a Google Doc, summarize what you observed and 2 changes you'd make. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

modules.title: Software Testing & Quality Assurance
modules.description: Write code you can trust — learn manual testing thinking and how to automate it.

children.title: Manual Testing Fundamentals
children.description: Think like a QA engineer — how to systematically break things on purpose.

links.title: How to Write Good Test Cases — Guru99
Submit your test case document
links.description: Pick any app you've built or use daily (e.g. a login form or a checkout flow). In a Google Sheet, write at least 10 test cases covering normal use, edge cases, and invalid input — each with steps, expected result, and pass/fail. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Writing Automated Unit Tests
children.description: Catch bugs automatically before they reach production.

links.title: Submit your unit-tested project repo
links.description: Add a pytest test suite to an existing Python project (e.g. your FastAPI backend) covering at least 8 test cases across at least 3 different functions or endpoints, including at least one test for an error case. Push to a public GitHub repo and include instructions for running the tests in the README. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Test-Driven Development
children.description: Write the test before the code — and see how it changes the way you design functions.

links.title: Test-Driven Development by Example — Key Ideas
Submit your TDD kata repo
links.description: Pick a small coding kata (e.g. FizzBuzz, a string reverser, a simple calculator) and build it strictly test-first: write a failing test, write the minimum code to pass it, refactor, repeat. Push the repo showing the commit history of this red-green-refactor cycle if possible, with a README explaining your process. Paste the repo URL here.

modules.title: Cybersecurity Fundamentals
modules.description: Understand how applications get attacked so you can build ones that don't.

children.title: Web Security Basics (OWASP Top 10)
children.description: The most common ways real web applications get exploited, and how to recognize them.

links.title: Submit your OWASP summary
links.description: In a Google Doc, pick 4 items from the OWASP Top 10 and explain in your own words: what the vulnerability is, a simple example of how it could be exploited, and one concrete way to prevent it in a real app. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Secure Coding Practices
children.description: Apply security thinking directly to the code you write every day.

links.title: Submit your hardened project repo
links.description: Take an existing backend project and harden it: add input validation on every endpoint, use parameterized queries (never string-concatenated SQL), and ensure passwords are hashed, not stored in plain text. Push to a public repo with a README listing exactly what you fixed and why. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Authentication & Authorization Deep Dive
children.description: Understand the difference between 'who are you' and 'what can you do' — and why mixing them up causes real breaches.

links.title: Submit your auth flow diagram and explanation
links.description: In a Google Doc, describe (in words or a simple diagram) how login and access control works in a project you've built or a real app you use — cover how the user is authenticated, how a session or token is issued, and how the backend decides what that user is allowed to do. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

modules.title: Career Growth & Workplace Skills
modules.description: Skills for after you get the job — surviving your first months, growing deliberately, and advocating for yourself.

children.title: Navigating Your First 90 Days
children.description: What to prioritize when you're new — before you're expected to already know everything.

links.title: The First 90 Days — Key Ideas Summary
How to Onboard Yourself at a New Job
Submit your 90-day plan
links.description: In a Google Doc, write a realistic 30-60-90 day plan for starting a new developer job — what you'd focus on learning in each phase, who you'd try to build relationships with, and how you'd know you're on track. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Finding and Working With a Mentor
children.description: Mentorship accelerates growth — learn to find one and actually make use of it.

links.title: How to Find a Mentor — Harvard Division of Continuing Education
Making the Most of a Mentorship — MIT
Submit your mentor outreach message
links.description: In a Google Doc, draft a genuine message you could send to someone you'd want as a mentor (a senior developer, a professor, an alum) asking for a short conversation. Then list 3 specific questions you'd want to ask them. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Performance Reviews & Self-Advocacy
children.description: Make sure your work actually gets noticed and credited.

links.title: How to Write a Brag Document — Julia Evans
How to Advocate for Yourself at Work — HBR
Submit your brag document
links.description: Start a 'brag document' in a Google Doc — a running list of things you've accomplished, with real specifics (what you built, what impact it had, what you learned). Add at least 5 real entries from your own work or coursework so far. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Building a Growth Mindset at Work
children.description: Treat being wrong, corrected, or out of your depth as normal, not as a threat.

links.title: Growth Mindset vs Fixed Mindset — Explained
Why Junior Developers Should Embrace Not Knowing Things
Submit your growth mindset reflection
links.description: In a Google Doc, write about a time you got something wrong or didn't know something in front of others (an interview, a code review, a class). Reflect on how you reacted in the moment versus how a growth-mindset response would look, and what you'd do differently next time. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

modules.title: Mobile App Development (React Native & Expo)
modules.description: Take your web development skills to iOS and Android using React Native and Expo.

children.title: React Native Fundamentals
children.description: Learn the core building blocks of React Native — components, styling, and state.

links.title: React Native Official Docs — Getting Started
Submit your first React Native app repo
links.description: Build a simple app with at least 3 screens using core components (View, Text, TextInput, FlatList, Image). Include navigation between screens (a simple button-based navigation is fine at this stage). Push to a public GitHub repo called 'rn-fundamentals' with a README explaining how to run it. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Expo Router & Navigation
children.description: Use Expo Router to build file-based navigation like a real production app.

links.title: Submit your Expo Router project repo
links.description: Rebuild or extend your previous app using Expo Router with at least: a tab layout, a stack navigator for a detail screen, and one dynamic route (e.g. [id].tsx). Push to a public GitHub repo called 'expo-router-practice' with a README. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Working with APIs & Local Storage in Mobile Apps
children.description: Fetch data from a real API and persist data locally on the device.

links.title: Networking in React Native — Official Docs
Submit your API-connected app repo
links.description: Build an app that fetches data from a free public API (e.g. https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com, https://openweathermap.org, or https://rickandmortyapi.com), displays it in a scrollable list, and lets the user save at least one item locally using AsyncStorage so it persists after closing the app. Push to a public GitHub repo with a README. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Native Device Features
children.description: Access the camera, location, and notifications using Expo's device APIs.

links.title: Submit a repo using at least one native device feature
links.description: Build a small app that uses at least one native device capability — camera, location, or local notifications. For example: a photo journal, a 'find my location' screen, or a reminder app that schedules a local notification. Push to a public GitHub repo with a README describing which feature you used and why. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Full Mobile App Project
children.description: Design, build, and ship a complete mobile app end to end.

links.title: Publishing to Expo / EAS Build Docs
Submit your full mobile app repo
links.description: Push your completed app to a public GitHub repo. It should combine navigation, an API or backend connection, and at least one native device feature or persisted local state. Include a README with setup instructions, screenshots, and a feature list. Paste the repo URL here.

links.title: Submit a 2-4 minute demo video
links.description: Record a screen recording (using your phone screen or a simulator) walking through your app's main features. Keep it under 4 minutes. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the URL here.

modules.title: Cloud & DevOps Fundamentals
modules.description: Learn how real applications get packaged, tested automatically, and deployed to the cloud.

children.title: Docker & Containerization
children.description: Package your applications so they run identically everywhere.

links.title: Submit your Dockerized project repo
links.description: Take any past project (e.g. your FastAPI backend) and write a Dockerfile for it. Build the image locally and confirm the container runs correctly. Push a public repo containing the Dockerfile, a .dockerignore, and a README with the exact build and run commands. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: CI/CD Basics
children.description: Automate testing and deployment so code ships safely and consistently.

links.title: Submit a repo with a working GitHub Actions workflow
links.description: Add a GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml) to any existing project that automatically runs on every push — at minimum it should install dependencies and run your test suite or a linter. Make sure the workflow passes (green check) at least once. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Cloud Deployment
children.description: Get a real application live on the internet using a modern cloud platform.

links.title: Submit your live deployed app URL
links.description: Deploy any working backend or full-stack project to a live URL using Render, Railway, Fly.io, or AWS. Confirm it's reachable and functional from a browser or Postman. Paste the live URL here (not the GitHub repo).

children.title: Monitoring & Logging Basics
children.description: Understand how teams know when something breaks in production before users complain.

links.title: Submit your logging setup repo
links.description: Add structured logging (e.g. Python's logging module or a library like structlog) to a backend project — log at minimum every incoming request and every error, with timestamps. Include a README explaining what you log and why. Paste the repo URL here.

modules.title: System Design Basics
modules.description: Understand how large-scale systems are designed — a core skill for technical interviews and senior roles.

children.title: Scalability Fundamentals
children.description: Learn the core vocabulary: horizontal vs vertical scaling, latency vs throughput, and CAP theorem.

links.title: Submit your scalability concepts summary
links.description: In a Google Doc, explain in your own words (300-400 words): horizontal vs vertical scaling, latency vs throughput, and the CAP theorem, with one real-world example for each. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Databases at Scale
children.description: Learn how databases handle huge amounts of data and traffic — sharding, replication, and indexing.

links.title: Use The Index, Luke — Indexing Deep Dive
Submit a database scaling case study
links.description: Pick one real company's public engineering blog post about scaling their database (search e.g. 'Instagram database sharding', 'Notion Postgres scaling'). Summarize in a Google Doc: what problem they had, what solution they used, and one trade-off they accepted. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Caching & Load Balancing
children.description: Speed up systems and distribute traffic across multiple servers.

links.title: Submit a repo demonstrating caching
links.description: Add a simple in-memory or Redis-based cache to any API endpoint in an existing project so repeated requests are served faster. Include a README showing response times before and after caching. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: System Design Interview Practice
children.description: Put it all together by designing a system end to end, the way you would in an interview.

links.title: Grokking the System Design Interview — Free Overview
Submit your system design write-up
links.description: Design one system of your choice (e.g. a URL shortener, a basic Twitter feed, or a ride-sharing app's core booking flow). In a Google Doc, cover: requirements, high-level architecture diagram (can be a simple drawing or described in text), database schema, and how you'd handle scale. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

links.title: Submit a video explaining your design
links.description: Record a 4-6 minute video walking through your system design write-up as if presenting it in an interview. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the URL here.

modules.title: Open Source Contribution
modules.description: Learn how open source works and make your first real contributions to public projects.

children.title: Understanding Open Source
children.description: Learn licenses, contribution etiquette, and how open source communities work.

links.title: Submit a summary of an open source project you admire
links.description: Pick one open source project you use or admire. In a Google Doc, write 200-300 words on: what it does, what license it uses, how contributions are submitted (check their CONTRIBUTING.md), and one thing you learned reading their codebase or issues. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Finding & Making Your First Contribution
children.description: Go from reading code to submitting a real pull request.

links.title: How to Write a Good Pull Request
Submit a link to your pull request
links.description: Find a 'good first issue' on a real open source project (docs fixes count!) and submit a pull request. It doesn't need to be merged yet — it just needs to be genuinely submitted and following the project's contribution guidelines. Paste the direct URL to your pull request (not the project's homepage).

children.title: Maintaining Your Own Open Source Project
children.description: Experience the other side — publish and maintain a small open source tool of your own.

links.title: Submit your open source project repo
links.description: Publish a small, genuinely useful tool or library (a CLI utility, a small npm/pip package, a code snippet collection) as a public repo. It must include: a clear README, a LICENSE file, and a CONTRIBUTING.md explaining how others could contribute. Paste the repo URL here.

modules.title: Data Science & Analytics Foundations
modules.description: Learn to work with real data — clean it, analyze it, visualize it, and build your first models.

children.title: Python for Data Analysis (Pandas & NumPy)
children.description: The two libraries every data analyst and scientist uses daily.

links.title: Submit your data cleaning notebook repo
links.description: Download any messy public dataset from Kaggle or data.gov.in. Using Pandas, clean it (handle missing values, fix data types, remove duplicates) and answer at least 5 questions about it using groupby, filtering, and aggregation. Push the Jupyter notebook and the dataset (or a link to it) to a public GitHub repo. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Data Visualization
children.description: Turn raw numbers into charts that actually communicate something.

links.title: Data Visualization Best Practices — Storytelling with Data
Submit your data visualization notebook repo
links.description: Using the dataset from the previous module (or a new one), create at least 4 different chart types (e.g. bar, line, scatter, histogram) using Matplotlib or Seaborn, each with clear titles and labels. Add a short written insight below each chart explaining what it shows. Push the notebook to a public GitHub repo. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Intro to Machine Learning
children.description: Understand the core ideas behind machine learning and train your first model.

links.title: Submit your first ML model repo
links.description: Using scikit-learn, train a simple classification or regression model on a beginner-friendly dataset (e.g. Titanic survival, Iris species, or house prices on Kaggle). Include: a train/test split, at least one evaluation metric (accuracy, RMSE, etc.), and a short written conclusion about how well it performed. Push the notebook to a public GitHub repo. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Data Science Mini Project
children.description: Combine everything into one polished, end-to-end analysis you can show off.

links.title: Submit your data science project repo
links.description: Pick a dataset and question you genuinely care about (sports stats, college placement data, weather, cricket scores — anything). Push a public repo containing: data cleaning, at least 3 visualizations, one simple model or statistical finding, and a README that reads like a mini case study (problem, approach, findings). Paste the repo URL here.

links.title: Submit a 3-5 minute walkthrough video
links.description: Record yourself walking through your findings as if presenting to a non-technical stakeholder — focus on the 'so what', not just the code. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the URL here.

modules.title: Freelancing & Personal Branding
modules.description: Build an online presence and learn how to win freelance work while still in college.

children.title: Building a Portfolio Website
children.description: Every developer needs one place that shows recruiters and clients what you can do.

links.title: How to Build a Developer Portfolio — freeCodeCamp
Submit your live portfolio site
links.description: Build and deploy a personal portfolio website (Vercel, Netlify, or GitHub Pages all work and are free) that includes: an about section, at least 3 real projects with links, your skills, and a way to contact you. Paste the live URL here.

children.title: Freelance Platforms & Proposals
children.description: Understand how freelance marketplaces work and how to write proposals that win work.

links.title: How to Write a Winning Upwork Proposal
Submit your freelance proposal drafts
links.description: In a Google Doc, write 2 freelance proposals for 2 different real job postings you find on Upwork, Fiverr, or a local freelance community — paste the job description above each proposal for context. Each proposal should be specific to that job, not generic. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Personal Branding & Content Creation
children.description: Build in public and let your work speak for itself online.

links.title: Build in Public — A Practical Guide
How to Write Technical Blog Posts Developers Actually Read
Submit a link to your technical blog post or thread
links.description: Write and publish one piece of technical content — a blog post (Hashnode, Dev.to, or Medium), a LinkedIn post, or an X/Twitter thread — explaining something you built or learned. Paste the direct published URL here.

modules.title: Technical Foundation
modules.description: Build the core technical skills every developer needs before writing their first real application.

children.title: Git & Version Control
children.description: Learn to track your code, collaborate with others, and never lose your work again.

links.title: Submit your GitHub profile
links.description: Create a GitHub account at github.com if you don't have one. Add a profile picture, your real name, and a short bio. Then copy your profile URL — it looks like github.com/yourusername — and paste it here.

links.title: Submit a repo with at least 3 meaningful commits
links.description: Create a new public repository on github.com, add a README.md explaining what the project is, and make at least 3 commits showing different stages of your work (not all at once). Paste the repo URL — github.com/yourusername/repo-name — here.

children.title: Linux & Command Line
children.description: Get comfortable with the terminal. Most dev environments and servers run Linux.

links.title: Submit a repo containing your shell scripts
links.description: Create a public GitHub repo called 'linux-practice'. Write at least 3 small bash scripts — for example: one that creates a folder structure, one that searches for a word in files, one that lists all .py files in a directory. Add a README explaining what each script does. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Python Fundamentals
children.description: Core Python every backend developer needs — variables, functions, loops, OOP, and file handling.

links.title: Automate the Boring Stuff with Python
Submit your Python fundamentals repo
links.description: Create a public GitHub repo called 'python-fundamentals'. Add Python files covering at least: variables and data types, loops and conditionals, functions with arguments and return values, a class with at least two methods, and reading/writing a text file. Each file should have comments explaining what the code does. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Data Structures & Algorithms
children.description: The concepts that show up in every technical interview and help you write faster, smarter code.

links.title: Submit your DSA implementations repo
links.description: Create a public GitHub repo called 'dsa-practice'. Implement from scratch in Python (no importing built-in data structure libraries): a stack, a queue, a singly linked list, binary search, and merge sort. Each should be in its own file with a short docstring and example usage. Include a README listing what is implemented. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: SQL & Databases
children.description: Write real queries, understand joins, and stop being afraid of databases.

links.title: Use The Index, Luke — SQL Performance
Submit your SQL mini-project repo
links.description: Create a public GitHub repo called 'sql-practice'. Include a single .sql file with: CREATE TABLE statements for a small project of your choice (e.g. a library, a student database, a shop), INSERT statements with at least 10 rows of sample data, and at least 6 SELECT queries using WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and a subquery. Add a README describing the project. Paste the repo URL here.

modules.title: Web & Backend Development
modules.description: Build real web applications. Go from HTML basics to shipping a working backend API.

children.title: HTML & CSS Basics
children.description: Build the visual layer of any web application. Learn how browsers render content and how to style it.

links.title: Submit your personal webpage repo
links.description: Build a personal webpage using only HTML and CSS — no JavaScript. It must include: a header with your name and a tagline, an about section, a list of skills, and a footer with links. Push to a public GitHub repo called 'my-webpage'. Enable GitHub Pages under Settings → Pages so the page is live at yourusername.github.io/my-webpage. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: JavaScript Fundamentals
children.description: Add interactivity to web pages and understand how the modern web actually works.

links.title: Submit your JavaScript mini-project repo
links.description: Build a small interactive project using vanilla JavaScript — ideas include a to-do list, a quiz app, a tip calculator, or a colour palette generator. The project must manipulate the DOM and respond to at least one user event. Push to a public GitHub repo with a README explaining what it does and how to open it locally. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: REST APIs & HTTP
children.description: Understand how the web communicates. Every backend and frontend developer needs this foundation.

links.title: What is a REST API? — RedHat
Submit your Postman collection repo
links.description: Use Postman to test a free public API — good choices are https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com or https://api.github.com. Make at least 5 requests covering GET with filters, POST with a request body, and a DELETE. In Postman, go to your collection → Export → save the .json file. Add it to a public GitHub repo called 'api-practice' with a README explaining each request and what response you got. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: FastAPI Backend Development
children.description: Build production-grade Python APIs with automatic docs, request validation, and clean structure.

links.title: Submit your FastAPI project repo
links.description: Build a FastAPI application with at least: 3 routes (one GET that returns a list, one POST that accepts a request body, one DELETE), Pydantic models for request and response validation, and an in-memory data store (a Python list or dict — no database needed yet). Push to a public GitHub repo with a README that includes setup instructions and example curl commands for each endpoint. Paste the repo URL here.

children.title: Full Backend Project
children.description: Ship a complete backend application — design, build, test, document, and demo it.

links.title: Writing a Good Project README — freeCodeCamp
Submit your project repo
links.description: Push your completed backend project to a public GitHub repo. The repo must include: a README with what the project does, how to run it locally (step by step), and a list of all API endpoints with example requests; a requirements.txt or pyproject.toml; and at least one PostgreSQL table with real data. Make sure the repo is public. Paste the repo URL here.

links.title: Submit a 2–4 minute demo video
links.description: Record a screen recording where you: briefly show your code structure (30 seconds), start the server, and then demo at least 3 API calls using Postman or the FastAPI /docs page. Keep it under 4 minutes. Free tools: Loom (loom.com) — records screen and webcam simultaneously with no editing needed. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the video URL here.

modules.title: Career Readiness
modules.description: Everything you need to present yourself confidently to recruiters and land your first tech job.

children.title: Build Your GitHub Profile
children.description: Your GitHub profile is your developer resume. Make it tell your story at a glance.

links.title: How to Create a GitHub Profile README
Submit your GitHub profile
links.description: Set up your GitHub profile: add a real profile picture, a bio, your location, and create a special repository named exactly your GitHub username (e.g. if your username is priyasharma, create a repo called priyasharma). Add a README.md to it that introduces who you are, what you're learning, and what you're building. Then paste your GitHub profile URL — github.com/yourusername — here.

children.title: Resume Writing
children.description: Write a one-page resume that gets past ATS filters and makes a recruiter want to call you.

links.title: Submit your resume as a Google Drive PDF
links.description: Create your resume using Google Docs, Overleaf, or Canva. Export or download it as a PDF. Upload the PDF to Google Drive, right-click it → Share → change access to 'Anyone with the link can view', and copy the shareable link. Make sure it opens without needing to sign in. Paste the link here.

children.title: LinkedIn Profile Setup
children.description: Build a LinkedIn presence that makes recruiters come to you.

links.title: How to Build a Strong LinkedIn Profile
Submit your LinkedIn profile URL
links.description: Complete your LinkedIn profile before submitting: add a professional photo, a headline (e.g. 'CS Student | Python & FastAPI | Building backend projects'), an About section of at least 3 sentences, your education, and at least 5 skills. Then go to your LinkedIn profile in a browser, copy the URL from the address bar — it looks like linkedin.com/in/yourname — and paste it here.

children.title: Interview Preparation
children.description: Crack technical and HR interviews. Understand what interviewers actually look for.

links.title: Submit a mock interview recording
links.description: Record yourself answering 3 interview questions out loud on camera — one technical (e.g. explain what a REST API is or how Python handles memory), one behavioural (e.g. tell me about a challenge you faced), and one HR (e.g. why do you want to work in tech?). Aim for 4–6 minutes total. No editing needed — genuine and clear is better than polished. Use Loom (loom.com) for easy recording. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the URL here.

children.title: Job Application Strategy
children.description: Apply smarter, not more. Learn where to find real jobs, how to write cold emails, and how to follow up.

links.title: How to Get Your First Developer Job — freeCodeCamp
Cold Email Templates for Job Seekers
Submit your job application tracker
links.description: Create a Google Sheet to track your job applications. Columns: Company, Role, Date Applied, How You Applied (portal/cold email/referral), Contact Name, Status, and Notes. Add at least 10 real companies you want to apply to — research each one and add a note on why it interests you. Go to File → Share → Anyone with the link can view, and paste the shareable link here.

modules.title: Soft Skills & Professional Growth
modules.description: The skills that determine how far you go once you get the job — communication, clarity, and professionalism.

children.title: Professional Email Writing
children.description: Write emails that get read and get responses. The single most used professional skill.

links.title: How to Write Professional Emails — Grammarly
Email Etiquette Rules Every Professional Should Know
Submit your email writing samples
links.description: Write 3 professional emails in a Google Doc: (1) a cold email to someone at a company you admire asking for a 15-minute call to learn about their work, (2) a follow-up email after applying for a job and hearing nothing for a week, (3) a thank-you email after a technical interview. Each email should have a subject line. Share the doc with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Communication Skills for Developers
children.description: Explain technical ideas clearly, ask good questions, and communicate well in teams.

links.title: How to Explain Technical Concepts Simply — freeCodeCamp
Submit a plain-language technical explanation video
links.description: Record a 2–3 minute video where you explain one technical concept to someone who has never coded — for example: what is a database, how does the internet work, or what is an API. Speak directly to camera. No slides needed — just clear, friendly explanation. Use Loom (loom.com) for easy no-edit recording. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the URL here.

children.title: Public Speaking & Presentations
children.description: Present your ideas confidently — in interviews, team standups, and project demos.

links.title: TED Talk — How to Speak so People Want to Listen
How to Give a Killer Presentation — HBR
Submit your presentation slides
links.description: Create a 5–8 slide presentation in Google Slides on any technical topic you have learned (e.g. what is Git and why it matters, how Python works, what is an API). Keep slides visual — one idea per slide, large text, minimal bullet points. Go to File → Share → Anyone with the link can view, and paste the shareable link here.

links.title: Submit a recorded presentation
links.description: Record yourself presenting your slides from the previous submission. Aim for 3–5 minutes. Use Loom (loom.com) — it records your screen and face at the same time with one click, no editing needed. Upload to YouTube as unlisted and paste the URL here.

children.title: Critical Thinking & Problem Solving
children.description: Develop the habit of breaking down hard problems, identifying root causes, and making reasoned decisions.

links.title: Mental Models for Problem Solving — FS Blog
Submit a structured problem breakdown
links.description: In a Google Doc, pick one real problem from your college or community — for example: students can't find internship opportunities, attendance registers are done on paper and get lost, or the library has no online search. Write: (1) a clear one-sentence problem statement, (2) who is affected and how, (3) the root causes (not just symptoms), and (4) three possible solutions with pros and cons of each. Aim for 400–600 words. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Time Management & Productivity
children.description: Do deep work, manage distractions, and build the habits that let you ship consistently.

links.title: Atomic Habits Key Ideas — James Clear
Submit your 4-week study plan
links.description: Create a Google Sheet with your study plan for the next 4 weeks. Structure it by day, with columns: Date, What I'll Study, Time Block (e.g. 6pm–8pm), Goal for the Session, and Did I Complete It (fill this in daily). Be realistic — plan around your college schedule. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Teamwork & Collaboration
children.description: Work well with others, handle disagreements professionally, and contribute meaningfully to a team.

links.title: Google's Project Aristotle — What Makes a Great Team
How to Give and Receive Feedback — Radical Candor
Submit a teamwork reflection
links.description: Write a 300–500 word reflection in a Google Doc about a time you worked in a team — in a college project, an event, a competition, or anywhere. Cover: what the goal was, what role you played, what went well, what was hard, one thing someone else did that helped the team, and what you would do differently. Share with 'Anyone with the link can view' and paste the link here.

children.title: Networking & Building Connections
children.description: Get comfortable reaching out to people in tech, building genuine connections, and asking for opportunities.

links.title: How to Network When You Hate Networking — HBR
How to Cold Message on LinkedIn Without Being Annoying
Submit a link to your LinkedIn post
links.description: Write and publish a post on LinkedIn about something real from your learning journey — a project you completed, a concept that finally clicked, a challenge you overcame, or something you built. Keep it genuine and 100–200 words. After posting, click the three dots (…) on your post → Copy link to post, and paste that link here. Do not paste your profile URL — paste the specific post URL.
